content.text: The fundamental distinction between the joint distribution p(x, y) and the conditional distribution p(y | x) underlies nearly every design decision in machine learning. Choosing which distribution to model determines what a system can do, what data it needs, and how it will fail. Understanding how these two distributions relate — and when the difference matters — is essential for reasoning about generative AI, probabilistic models, and the limits of any learned system.
For random variables X (input) and Y (output or label), three distributions are in play:
Probability of x and y co-occurring
Probability of y given x is observed
Probability of x (or y) alone, integrating out the other
They are linked by the product rule and Bayes' theorem:

attrs.latex: p(x, y) = p(y \mid x)\, p(x) = p(x \mid y)\, p(y)

content.text: A model of the joint p(x, y) implicitly contains both conditionals p(y | x) and p(x | y), and both marginals p(x) and p(y). A model of the conditional p(y | x) contains none of the others — it is the minimum sufficient quantity for classification, and nothing more.
What Each Distribution Lets You Do

import torch
import torch.distributions as D


# ── Example: 2D Gaussian joint distribution ──────────────────────────────────
#
# Suppose X = pixel brightness, Y = class label (cat=0, dog=1)
# Joint: p(x, y) = p(x | y) * p(y)
# Conditional: p(y | x) derived via Bayes

class JointGaussian:
    """
    Illustrative joint distribution p(x, y) for a 2-class problem.
    Generative model: models both p(x | y) and p(y).
    """
    def __init__(self):
        # Prior over classes (dogs are more common)
        self.p_y = D.Categorical(probs=torch.tensor([0.4, 0.6]))
        # Class-conditional distributions p(x | y)
        self.p_x_given_y = [
            D.Normal(loc=torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0]), scale=torch.tensor([0.5, 0.5])),  # cats
            D.Normal(loc=torch.tensor([3.0, 1.0]), scale=torch.tensor([0.5, 0.5])),  # dogs
        ]

    def sample(self, n: int):
        """Sample from joint: first draw y, then x | y."""
        y = self.p_y.sample((n,))    # (n,) class labels
        x = torch.stack([
            self.p_x_given_y[y_i.item()].sample() for y_i in y
        ])                            # (n, 2) inputs
        return x, y

    def log_joint(self, x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """log p(x, y) = log p(x | y) + log p(y)"""
        log_p_y = self.p_y.log_prob(y)   # (n,)
        log_p_x_given_y = torch.stack([
            self.p_x_given_y[y_i.item()].log_prob(x[i]).sum()
            for i, y_i in enumerate(y)
        ])                                # (n,)
        return log_p_x_given_y + log_p_y

    def log_conditional(self, x: torch.Tensor, y_query: int) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        log p(y=y_query | x) via Bayes' theorem.
        Requires both class-conditional likelihoods — not possible without the
        full joint model.
        """
        log_priors = self.p_y.logits - torch.logsumexp(self.p_y.logits, dim=0)
        log_likelihoods = torch.stack([
            dist.log_prob(x).sum() for dist in self.p_x_given_y
        ])  # (K,)
        log_joint_per_class = log_likelihoods + log_priors
        log_p_x = torch.logsumexp(log_joint_per_class, dim=0)
        return log_joint_per_class[y_query] - log_p_x  # Bayes denominator

    def sample_x_given_y(self, y: int, n: int = 1) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Sample new inputs for a specific class — generation."""
        return self.p_x_given_y[y].sample((n,))
Why the Joint Is Strictly More Informative
The joint p(x, y) contains strictly more information than p(y | x). From the joint you can derive:
p(y | x) by normalising over y — for classification.
p(x | y) by normalising over x — for conditional generation ("draw me a cat").
p(x) by marginalising over y — for density estimation and anomaly detection.
p(y) by marginalising over x — the marginal class distribution.
p(x_missing | x_observed, y) — imputation under the model.
From p(y | x) alone, none of the above are derivable. The conditional is the minimum sufficient statistic for the prediction task, and its compactness is exactly what makes it efficient to learn.
The Price of Modelling the Full Joint

Modelling p(x, y) correctly requires the model to represent the marginal p(x) — the full complexity of the input space. For high-dimensional inputs like images, text, or audio, this is an enormous challenge: the model must capture all modes of variation in x whether or not that variation is relevant to predicting y. Features like lighting direction in images, rare synonyms in text, or background noise in audio contribute to p(x) but may be irrelevant to the classification task.

This means that a generative classifier — which uses p(y | x) derived via Bayes from a joint model — can be beaten by a discriminative classifier that models p(y | x) directly, simply because the discriminative model wastes no capacity on irrelevant variation in x. This is the mathematical basis for Vapnik's principle: "do not solve a harder problem as an intermediate step to solving an easier one."
Despite this argument for discriminative models, there are situations where the joint model wins:
Unlabelled examples can train p(x); the marginal class prior p(y) informs classification
p(y) is part of the model; rare-class priors are explicitly represented
p(y | x_observed) = Σ_{x_missing} p(y | x) · p(x_missing | x_observed) — requires joint
Low p(x) at test time signals OOD — not possible with p(y | x) alone
"What would this x look like if y were different?" requires p(x | y)
The Special Case: Unsupervised Generative Models

When there are no labels (y is absent), the only distribution to model is the marginal p(x). Language models, image generative models, and audio synthesis models are all in this category. The objective is:

Here the joint/conditional distinction collapses: there is only the marginal p(x) to model. Conditioning can be introduced post-hoc — a conditional generation model p(x | c) where c is a text prompt, class label, or style specification is still fundamentally a generative model of p(x, c) marginalised appropriately. GPT-style models learn p(x_{1:T}) as a joint over token sequences, but any prefix can serve as a conditioning context.
Bayes-Optimal Classifiers: Both Representations Are Equivalent

With infinite data and a perfectly expressive model, the Bayes-optimal classifier from a joint model and from a conditional model are identical — both converge to the true p(y | x). The difference is purely in the estimation problem given finite data. A model of p(x, y) has more parameters to estimate (it must also fit p(x)), leading to higher variance but also capturing more structure. A model of p(y | x) has fewer free parameters and lower variance but cannot extrapolate to generative tasks.

import torch


def bayes_optimal_from_joint(
    log_p_x_given_y: torch.Tensor,   # (n, K) log p(x | y=k) for each sample
    log_p_y: torch.Tensor,           # (K,) log p(y=k)
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Compute p(y | x) via Bayes' theorem from a joint generative model.
    Same result as a discriminative model in the large-data limit.

    log_p_x_given_y: (n, K) — one score per sample per class
    log_p_y:         (K,)   — log prior over classes
    returns:         (n, K) — posterior p(y | x)
    """
    # log p(x, y=k) = log p(x | y=k) + log p(y=k)
    log_joint = log_p_x_given_y + log_p_y.unsqueeze(0)   # (n, K)
    # log p(y=k | x) = log p(x, y=k) - log p(x)
    #                 = log_joint - logsumexp over k
    log_p_x = torch.logsumexp(log_joint, dim=1, keepdim=True)  # (n, 1)
    log_posterior = log_joint - log_p_x                        # (n, K)
    return log_posterior.exp()                                 # (n, K)


# Both approaches predict the same class under identical generative assumptions:
# argmax_y p(y | x)  ==  argmax_y [log p(x | y) + log p(y)]  (Bayes)
# argmax_y f_theta(x)[y]                                       (discriminative)
#
# They differ only in how much structure is assumed / estimated from data.

Mental model: the joint p(x, y) is the complete blueprint of the data — it specifies everything about how inputs and labels co-occur. The conditional p(y | x) is derived from this blueprint by fixing x and asking which labels are plausible. Modelling the joint is like building a complete city map; modelling the conditional is like memorising only the fastest route from A to B. The route is all you need for navigation, but the full map enables many more queries — the trade-off is how much effort it takes to build.

attrs.alt: Diagram comparing joint distribution p(X,Y) enabling generation, sampling, and Bayes theorem, versus conditional p(Y|X) enabling direct classification with lower model complexity

content.text: The joint and conditional formulations each offer distinct advantages, and the right choice depends on whether you need the model to generate new data, explain inputs, or simply classify them accurately.

enables data generation and augmentation; allows density estimation (is this input in-distribution?); provides a unified model for any derived query via marginalisation or conditioning; enables semi-supervised learning by using unlabelled data to fit p(x).

lower model complexity since p(x) need not be captured; stronger classification accuracy per labelled sample; faster inference without need to estimate the input distribution; less prone to mode collapse in high-dimensional input spaces.

models like BERT learn a conditional p(masked_token | context) but can later be fine-tuned for classification; this gives discriminative performance with representation quality from generative pretraining.
How to Implement: Joint and Conditional Models

The following code demonstrates implementing both a generative (Naive Bayes, which models the joint) and a discriminative (Logistic Regression, which models the conditional) classifier on the same dataset, then using the generative model for tasks beyond classification.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB       # generative: models p(x|y) and p(y)
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression  # discriminative: models p(y|x)
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification

X, y = make_classification(n_samples=500, n_features=20, n_informative=10,
                            n_classes=2, random_state=42)
X_train, X_test = X[:400], X[400:]
y_train, y_test = y[:400], y[400:]

# --- Generative model: Gaussian Naive Bayes ---
# Internally fits p(x|y=0), p(x|y=1) and p(y)
gnb = GaussianNB()
gnb.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f"Naive Bayes accuracy:    {gnb.score(X_test, y_test):.3f}")

# Joint model capabilities beyond classification:
# 1. Class-conditional log-likelihood (is this sample likely under class 0?)
log_p_x_given_y0 = gnb._joint_log_likelihood(X_test[:1])[:, 0]
print(f"log p(x|y=0): {log_p_x_given_y0[0]:.2f}")

# 2. Marginal likelihood p(x) — useful for anomaly detection
log_p_x = np.log(np.exp(gnb._joint_log_likelihood(X_test[:1])).sum(axis=1))
print(f"log p(x): {log_p_x[0]:.2f}")

# --- Discriminative model: Logistic Regression ---
# Directly optimises p(y|x); ignores p(x)
lr = LogisticRegression(max_iter=500)
lr.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f"Logistic Regression accuracy: {lr.score(X_test, y_test):.3f}")

# Only classification is possible — no density estimation
probs = lr.predict_proba(X_test[:3])
print(f"P(y=1|x) for first 3 test samples: {probs[:, 1].round(3)}")

# Example 1: Gaussian Mixture Model as joint density estimator
import numpy as np
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture
import torch

# Two-class 2D dataset
np.random.seed(42)
X_c0 = np.random.randn(200, 2) + [0, 0]    # class 0 centered at origin
X_c1 = np.random.randn(200, 2) + [3, 3]    # class 1 centered at (3,3)
X    = np.vstack([X_c0, X_c1])
y    = np.array([0]*200 + [1]*200)

# Fit per-class GMMs to model p(x | y)
gmm0 = GaussianMixture(n_components=1).fit(X[y == 0])
gmm1 = GaussianMixture(n_components=1).fit(X[y == 1])
p_y  = np.array([0.5, 0.5])   # prior: equal classes

def joint_classify(x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """Classify via p(y|x) = p(x|y)*p(y) / Z using joint model."""
    log_p_x_given_0 = gmm0.score_samples(x)
    log_p_x_given_1 = gmm1.score_samples(x)
    log_unnorm = np.stack([
        log_p_x_given_0 + np.log(p_y[0]),
        log_p_x_given_1 + np.log(p_y[1])
    ], axis=1)
    # Softmax over classes for p(y|x)
    log_z    = np.logaddexp(log_unnorm[:, 0], log_unnorm[:, 1])
    p_y_given_x = np.exp(log_unnorm - log_z[:, None])
    return p_y_given_x

test_pt = np.array([[1.5, 1.5]])
probs   = joint_classify(test_pt)
print(f"P(y=0|x=1.5,1.5): {probs[0,0]:.3f}")
print(f"P(y=1|x=1.5,1.5): {probs[0,1]:.3f}")

# Anomaly detection — only possible with joint model
log_p_x_0 = gmm0.score_samples(test_pt)
log_p_x_1 = gmm1.score_samples(test_pt)
log_p_x   = np.logaddexp(log_p_x_0 + np.log(0.5), log_p_x_1 + np.log(0.5))
print(f"log p(x): {log_p_x[0]:.3f}  (lower = more anomalous)")

# Example 2: How discriminative BERT fine-tuning gives p(y|x) only
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch, torch.nn.functional as F

# BERT fine-tuned for sentiment is a discriminative model p(y|x)
# It cannot generate new text or compute p(x) — only p(y|x)
model_name = "distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english"
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model      = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(model_name)

text    = "The movie had stunning visuals but a disappointing plot."
inputs  = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    logits = model(**inputs).logits           # raw class scores

probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
labels = ["NEGATIVE", "POSITIVE"]
print(f"p(NEGATIVE | text): {probs[0,0]:.3f}")
print(f"p(POSITIVE | text): {probs[0,1]:.3f}")

# A conditional model cannot answer: "Generate a text like this" or
# "How likely is this text under the data distribution?"
# For generation, you would need a joint model (GPT, T5, etc.)
Real-World Example: Generative vs Discriminative in Practice

Consider building a content moderation system for a social media platform. The platform needs to classify whether a post is toxic (conditional task) AND detect novel attack patterns not seen during training (requires density estimation from joint model). This dual need motivates a hybrid architecture.

# Hybrid: use generative model for OOD detection + discriminative for classification
import numpy as np
from sklearn.covariance import EllipticEnvelope  # models p(x) for anomaly detection
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Simulated text feature vectors (in practice from a sentence encoder)
np.random.seed(0)
X_normal = np.random.randn(1000, 64)            # normal posts
y_labels  = (X_normal[:, 0] > 0).astype(int)   # toxic if first feature > 0

# Discriminative model for classification
clf = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000).fit(X_normal, y_labels)

# Generative component: model p(x) to detect anomalies
ood_detector = EllipticEnvelope(contamination=0.05).fit(X_normal)

def moderate(features: np.ndarray) -> dict:
    """Classify post and flag if input is out-of-distribution."""
    is_in_dist = ood_detector.predict(features) == 1   # 1 = inlier
    prob_toxic  = clf.predict_proba(features)[:, 1]
    return {
        "p_toxic":       prob_toxic.round(3).tolist(),
        "in_distribution": is_in_dist.tolist(),
        "action": ["flag_for_review" if not ind else
                   ("remove" if pt > 0.9 else "allow")
                   for ind, pt in zip(is_in_dist, prob_toxic)]
    }

# Normal post
normal_post = np.random.randn(1, 64)
# Novel attack pattern (out-of-distribution)
ood_post    = np.random.randn(1, 64) * 5 + 10  # far from training distribution

print("Normal post:  ", moderate(normal_post))
print("OOD attack:   ", moderate(ood_post))
# OOD attack gets flagged for review even if discriminative model seems confident
Choosing a generative model when you only need classification.
Generative classifiers require modelling the full input distribution, which needs more data and compute than discriminative models for the same classification accuracy.
Assuming conditional models can detect distribution shift.

A discriminative model p(y|x) produces confident predictions even for out-of-distribution inputs because it learns only the decision boundary, not the input density. Always pair with density estimation for OOD detection.
p(x, y) is not the same as p(x) or p(y). Always check which quantity a model outputs before using it in downstream computations.
Computing p(x) from a discriminative model.

Logistic regression and neural classifiers do not provide p(x). Attempting to normalise p(y|x) over inputs does not give the marginal — the denominator integral over x is intractable without the joint model.
Using generative pretraining losses for discriminative fine-tuning without adjustment.

LLM pretraining minimises p(x) via next-token prediction (joint over the sequence). Fine-tuning for classification optimises p(y|x). The two objectives are related but distinct — fine-tuning requires adapting the loss function.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# WRONG: using discriminative model for OOD detection
X_train = np.random.randn(200, 2)
y_train = (X_train[:, 0] > 0).astype(int)
lr = LogisticRegression().fit(X_train, y_train)

ood_sample = np.array([[100.0, 100.0]])  # far outside training distribution
wrong_confidence = lr.predict_proba(ood_sample)[0, 1]
print(f"LR confidence on OOD sample: {wrong_confidence:.3f}")
# ~1.0 — wrongly very confident because it cannot model p(x)

# RIGHT: use marginal density from generative model for OOD check
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture
gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=2).fit(X_train)
log_p_x_train = gmm.score_samples(X_train).mean()
log_p_x_ood   = gmm.score_samples(ood_sample)[0]
print(f"Average log p(x) in training:   {log_p_x_train:.2f}")
print(f"log p(x) for OOD sample:        {log_p_x_ood:.2f}")  # much lower
# Now you can threshold on log_p_x to reject OOD inputs before classification
Use discriminative models as the default for classification.
Logistic regression, gradient-boosted trees, and neural classifiers achieve higher accuracy per labelled sample than generative classifiers because they don't waste capacity modelling p(x).
Add a density estimator for OOD detection.
Pair any discriminative classifier with a lightweight p(x) estimator (GMM, Isolation Forest, or a normalising flow) to flag inputs outside the training distribution.
The BERT/GPT recipe: pretrain to learn p(x) implicitly, then fine-tune a discriminative head. This leverages both paradigms' strengths.
Normalise carefully when using Bayes' theorem.
When computing p(y|x) = p(x|y)p(y) / p(x), ensure the numerator sums to 1 over y by using log-sum-exp rather than direct probability products to avoid numerical underflow.
Evaluate separately on in-distribution and OOD test sets.
A model's accuracy on held-out IID data does not predict its behaviour on novel inputs. Always include OOD evaluation for production deployments.

The computational gap between joint and conditional models widens dramatically with input dimensionality. A discriminative model trained on 1M image-label pairs needs to capture only the decision boundary in pixel space. A generative model needs to represent the full distribution over high-dimensional images — requiring orders of magnitude more parameters or data.
Provides both discriminative (LogisticRegression, SVC, RandomForest) and generative (GaussianNB, GaussianMixture) models with unified .fit/.predict APIs.
For deep discriminative models (classification networks) and deep generative models (VAEs, GANs, diffusion) respectively.
Normalising flows for exact density estimation p(x) with tractable likelihoods.
LLM APIs provide discriminative outputs (completion probabilities, logprobs) and generative capabilities (sampling) from the same joint model.
Collection of density-based and distance-based OOD detectors that can be paired with discriminative classifiers.
Low — must model all of X

Neural discriminative models are well-known to produce high-confidence predictions on adversarial and OOD inputs. This is not a bug in the model but a fundamental consequence of not modelling p(x). Always add input validation or density thresholding in safety-critical applications.
Joint generative models trained on sensitive data may memorise and reproduce training examples. Differential privacy during training (DP-SGD) provides formal guarantees but reduces model quality.

A discriminative model trained on distribution A and deployed on distribution B may silently degrade without warning. Monitor input feature statistics and compare to training statistics in production.
When computing p(y|x) = p(x|y)p(y)/p(x) with probability products, values underflow to zero for high-dimensional inputs. Always compute in log-space using log-sum-exp.

The joint p(x, y) = p(y | x) · p(x) = p(x | y) · p(y) subsumes all three other distributions; the conditional p(y | x) is the minimum needed for classification and cannot be inverted to recover p(x, y).
Discriminative models estimate p(y | x) directly — efficient but cannot generate x, detect OOD inputs, or handle missing features without extra machinery.
Generative models estimate p(x, y) or p(x) — richer but harder to estimate; the benefit appears in low-data regimes, semi-supervised settings, and generative tasks.
With infinite data, both approaches converge to the same Bayes-optimal classifier; the difference is finite-sample estimation efficiency.

Modern large language models train on the unsupervised objective log p(x) over unlabelled text, learning the full joint over token sequences — making them generative models that can be steered into discriminative behaviour at inference time.
